item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's not a format screw, if you're talking about the whole containment class and whatnot. It's in plenty of articles like SCP-4971.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Tfw the fancy format screw at the top of the article makes you cringe, and you get ready to slog through a tryhard skip, but it's f%g great.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't know anything about the Sloth's Pit canon, so it took me a while to figure out that I wasn't getting an explanation of what the Goatman was. I also have never seen Gravity Falls.

I enjoyed the writing of the article proper, but the logs seemed sort of like a tonal shift for me. The article proper gave me the impression of a creepy, mysterious, or horrifying object, and I was geared up for that. But the logs were more goofy and whimsical, which is fine (although not my personal preference), but it seemed abrupt.

I also disliked the characters of Bob and Alice. Their dialogue felt forced, cliched, and kind of irritating. Kind of like every "goofy reckless male character/uptight responsible female character" dynamic from a mid-2000s children's film or cartoon. They did grow on me a bit by the end, but probably not enough to read the tale linked at the end (sorry). I feel like you could make their dynamic better for me by adding some interactions which don't play into that dynamic, but I seem to be the only person in this thread who disliked it so you probably shouldn't.

I was debating whether to upvote because the writing was good or no-vote because I didn't personally care for the characters or tone, but ultimately I just no-voted. Maybe I'll read some more articles in this canon and revisit this one later to see if I enjoy it more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You could have at least tried to make it better.

If you're going to criticize articles, please try to do so respectfully.

SCPs that use exploration logs as their centrepiece are quite common, with SCP-093 being the foremost example. You are free to dislike such SCPs if you so choose, but there's nothing objectively wrong with them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The Logs were pretty cool. But the SCP article itself is very weak. You could have at least tried to make it better. But it seemed like you were more interested in using your tale as a backbone rather than making a SCP that can stand on its own. -1

item.title: SANTA
item.content:encoded: ;-; but santa

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just wondering, the distance they fell was merely at least 100km. Could be they were dragged down much faster than the usual ~200km/h terminal velocity. As China was but a few metres below the bottom of the pit, wouldn't about 13,000km of line be more likely to find the bottom?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That was amazing, but I'm concerned that by further referencing… the entity… you may be giving it a dangerous increase in pataphysical power.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One might even say it's a … plot hole…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I recognize you sir. I've seen your statue in the unforbidden dog park…

stops reading, slams his hands down on the desk, pushes himself up to his feet, and paces around the room with his hands alternatively thrusting into the air and flopping down at his sides, and an exasperated look on his face for at least two solid minutes

What can I possibly say in response to that, other than… Good Night.

Update: No, actually? I take that back. I do have one other response to this skip:

According to 4040's rules, 4040 can be contained by breaking all of 4040's rules. I humbly present for your consideration…

Spitting Distance And Velocity

I regret everything.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Also I almost instantly started hearing Tofflemire's lines in Soos's voice. Anybody else get that?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is giving off slight Skippy Dee vibes, but still okay.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"In this land of ours there are many great bits,
but none more bottomless than the Bottomless Pit!

Which, as you can see here, is bottomless."

Dammit the skip beat me to it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is good SCP. I +1-d it, but I am miffed that this is not Santa related.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: “Question: is it bottomless?” Is more then a gag/throwaway line in this context.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nice SCP, disappointed it wasn't Santa.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd never looked at the S&C Plastics canon before, but this entry 100% sold me on it. Enthusiastic +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Gonna be honest… I +1ed the moment I saw it tied into S&C Plastics, and when I got to the end I wished I could do it again.

It's true that for the contest a little more delving into the narrative nature of history would be nice, but as it is I still like it a lot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If someone tries to break them up, their name had damn well better be Eve. Falling all the way through Sloth's Pit is bound to leave enough narrative potential on them to pull off that bit of sillyness.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The Chekov's Gun here was the Goatman becoming real and the Foundation dealing with it,

Ah, within the canon he's kind of a regular character and not particularly sinister. I can see why that felt like a fake-out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Never read sloth pit cannon before, but I will definitely check it out. Love the humor and references (I HAVE BEEN FALLING FOR 30 MINUTES). My only problem is that I personally think that an SCP as important as a multiple of 1000 should be able to fit around most cannons without the need for background reading. Novote

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I just noticed a small grammatical error:

…the things we dream and fear, all of that effects our thoughts and feelings,

Should be:

…the things we dream and fear, all of that affects our thoughts and feelings,

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If I understand the SCP correctly, I like the idea of there being this pit that brings stories/ideas to life, so here's my +1.

"In this land of ours, there are many great pits, but none more bottomless than the bottomless pit, which as you can see here is bottomless."

The memories of Gravity Falls (assuming you were referencing that show).

Also, since I've been browsing a certain site for a while, I can't help but think of a specific trope for Alice and Bob.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 for "the unforbidden dog park"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Great job creating an interesting origin story for Sloth's Pit that's still accessible for folks who aren't familiar with the canon! I thought the dialogue between Alice and Bob got a bit too banter-riffic at points, but it works with the overall tone you've got going. The conflict resolved itself more quickly than I'd like, but I still enjoyed this enough to happily upvote it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"In this land of ours, there are many great pits, but none more bottomless than the bottomless pit, which as you can see here is bottomless."

Nice.

As a contest entry, I'd argue the history here is a tad watered down, as it seems to be a mere vehicle to reach the good stuff at the bottom of the pit. Had there been maybe more logs about the impact of finding this pit on the people of the town over time, I feel the article would be a little more within the spirit of the contest.

That said, the anomaly itself is quite interesting and I really like the article as a welcome addition to S&C plastics that not only helps to explain why Sloth's Pit is the way it is but also has potential implications for other nexuses.

Either way, it's a solid article. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was an overall enjoyable article, even if the banter between the two agents crossed into aggravating very quickly. Nonetheless, it's a cute idea and well-executed and the image of a narrative black hole is a good one.

The main issue I have is that the connection with history is just "it's the founder of the town down there." This is a huge missed opportunity, imo, as the discipline of history is the creation of narratives of how things happened. There's a lot that the author could have done to address "history," rather than "events that happened in the past," and I would have happily upvoted that article for 4k. As is, no-vote for now, probably upvote after the contest.

item.title: Tofflemire
item.content:encoded:
I like this, I really do, but this is Mr Tofflemire's job and in my head Canon the foundation punishes those who misbehave in sensitive situations. Him just fooling around on the job just to make some references and Jokes. Maybe have him reprimanded at the end of video 3. (Unless, of course, he is an established character in the S and C Plastics group which my searches couldn't find.)

Anyway, I dig the article.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Tofflemire: Because… we needed to fall down SCP-4000 to move the plot forward.

I think if this was anything else besides Sloths Pit I would have downvoted right there, heh.

Admittedly, my knowledge of Sloths Pit is limited (only read ihp's prop and have a basic knowledge of the concept), but I really enjoyed this. The characters are great, I really dig the giant spooky man dragging them down the hole, I like the Foundation's reaction to stuff going on here ("don't fucking talk about it, you'll make it worse!"), and I really liked that little rhyme at the end.

But really, let's face it. The +1 here is for digging so deep you reach China.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I fucking dig(ayy) this.

It's pretty incredible to me, how well this segues from and builds off ihp's S&C work - so much so that, even though I knew going into it that it wasn't written by ihp, that fact completely left me halfway through and I ended up hopping into chat to inform ihp of a grammatical error (fixed).

It may not have the feel of historicity something pertaining to the Foundation as a whole would, but as something tied into a specific canon, it does its job splendidly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really enjoyed this. I expected something a bit darker, but I got a slightly concerning but funny piece. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Something about this was just…perfect.

I've got a weakness for agents like Bob + Alice. Mostly because Denton and McDowell act the same way on occasion.

4000-1 was great. I loved how…serious and yet, nonsensical it was. It was tight enough that it didn't drag on, and I enjoyed it.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Plot hole

You know what, fuck it, that's enough to make me vote for this.

A literal plot hole. Heh.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I guess you could call it a genuine plot hole.

OH NO

Why? D:

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A pit that generates narratives, eh? I guess you could call it a genuine plot hole.

+1

Great addition to the S&C Plastics as well as a more whimsical approach to Pataphysics in the style of American Gods or Discworld.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I ship the two agents. Is it weird that I ship them? Probably. Regardless, I'm definitely giving a +1 for a good narrative. The Gravity Falls reference and general lack of seriousness from Agent Bob amused me, and he made a good foil for Agent Alice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel dumb because it took me until footnote 4 to realize this is the Sloth's pit of Sloth's Pit. :D Hot damn!

That said, I'm a little unsure about the part stating that SP's general effects are narrative in origin. That was only just established in that -001, and I don't think it's the kind of thing that should be taken as a given.

But if nothing else, this is told really well, and I am for once not disappointed to get 'the answer' behind a big piece of site lore.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wouldn't say the gun never fired. In fact, I think the point is they're deliberately not firing it. And the Foundation actually learning things from the stuff they contain is a rare sight.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel the same way. I want to like this, but it just frustrates me.

See Below

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've downvoted the other 4K submissions so far, but I leave this one at a novote.

The whole thing is really well written, and works (I think) as a good intro to the Sloth's Pit canon of which I've read very, very little. That being said, I was definitely expecting more of a climax.

The Chekov's Gun here was the Goatman becoming real and the Foundation dealing with it, but that gun was never fired. It leaves space open for more, but… I feel like the "more" should already be here.

I feel quite conflicted about this piece.

item.title: Here's hoping this does better than my 3kcon entry
item.content:encoded:
Sorry it took me so long to post here.

I'm glad a lot of people are liking this. I kind of deliberately avoided writing a Herman Fuller 4000 entry just because that would have been utterly predictable, though if I wanted to I probably could have thought of something since the Circus is old enough and Herman no doubt has had his fingers in a lot of pies over the years.

After Dread & Circuses and War On All Fronts (which I avoided here since we used that for our Doomcon entry), S&C Plastics is the Canon I have the most experience with, so I decided to write an SCP about the hole in the ground that started it all. A Nexus were anomalies all revolved around narration seemed like it had a lot of potential for a 4kcon entry.

Thanks to IHP especially, as well as to everyone else who read this over in chat.
